summary: Gruber explains why Apple would double the iPhone's display resolution to 960×640, responding to reader skepticism about the need for a better screen. He argues that while the existing 162 ppi iPhone display is good compared to other electronics, it's still crude compared to print. The new resolution of roughly 325-330 ppi would rival the optical quality of glossy magazines, making on-screen typography nearly indistinguishable from print. He also notes that Android's higher-resolution AMOLED displays are undermined by poor typefaces and sub-pixel limitations. On the developer side, he speculates that pixel-doubled apps would look identical to current ones and that MobileSafari would likely adopt virtual pixels to maintain layout compatibility.
key_points: The benchmark Apple is targeting isn't competing devices but the optical quality of printed magazines — roughly 325-330 ppi would make pixels essentially invisible to the naked eye
Android's 800×480 AMOLED displays have a resolution advantage over the current iPhone, but this is undermined by low-quality typefaces and fewer sub-pixels per pixel

Doubling resolution in each dimension (quadrupling total pixels) means existing apps can run pixel-doubled and look physically identical on the smaller screen, unlike the iPad's problematic pixel-doubling
MobileSafari will likely need to introduce virtual pixels ('px' mapping to 2×2 physical pixel squares) to maintain web layout compatibility across old and new devices
money_quotes: The next-gen iPhone is shooting for that caliber of resolution — not merely to exceed the resolution of competing devices, but to rival the optical quality of print.

Compare type on your iPhone or iPod Touch against the type in a glossy magazine. You can see pixels and anti-aliasing smudginess on an iPhone. You can't see "dots" in the letterforms of a printed magazine.
There will be four pixels packed into the space now occupied by a single pixel.

The total number of pixels are indeed quadrupled, but I think "double" is the better word to express the increase in resolution in common sense terms. Pixels-per-inch is the appropriate unit of measure here, and that's what Apple is set to double.

If existing unmodified apps run pixel-doubled, they should look identical with the naked eye to how they look today on existing iPhone displays — not at all like when iPhone apps are pixel-doubled on the iPad.
The effect is sort of like when you print a screen-optimized font like Chicago.
key_insight: Apple's goal with the doubled iPhone resolution isn't to win a spec war against Android but to cross the threshold where screen typography becomes indistinguishable from print.
